title: Sideroxylon repens
taxonomy: Sideroxylon repens is classified within the family Sapotaceae. The genus Sideroxylon is part of the Sapotaceae family. This species is native to tropical and subtropical regions of the Americas. Sideroxylon repens is also known as the creeping sapote. Further classification of Sideroxylon repens includes its placement within the order Ericales.
uses: Sideroxylon repens, also known as the creeping satinwood, has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits. The plant's roots are rich in saponins, which are known to have anti-inflammatory and antibacterial properties. Traditionally, the roots of Sideroxylon repens have been used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and stomach ulcers. In some cultures, the plant's extracts have been used to treat fever and other respiratory infections. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy of Sideroxylon repens in treating these conditions.

intro: Sideroxylon repens, also known as the Creeping Elephant Tree, is a perennial plant species belonging to the Sapotaceae family, characterized by its unique, creeping habit and small, white flowers. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various health conditions, including fever, inflammation, and digestive issues, due to its potential anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Sideroxylon repens include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic compounds, such as sidagin, sidagenin, and quercetin, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is often prepared as a decoction or tincture, or used as an extract to make capsules, infusions, and teas.
